questions.question: Which war is known as 'The Great War'?
questions.explanation: World War I (1914–1918) was known as 'The Great War' at the time, before World War II redefined the scale of global conflict. It involved most of Europe's major powers, killed an estimated 20 million people, and introduced industrialized warfare including trench warfare, chemical weapons, and aerial bombing.
questions.memory_hook: The Great War = WWI. It was so terrible, they called it 'the war to end all wars' — then WWII happened.

questions.question: Which explorer first circumnavigated the globe solo?
questions.explanation: Joshua Slocum completed the first solo circumnavigation of the globe by sea from 1895 to 1898, aboard his boat Spray. He wrote 'Sailing Alone Around the World' (1900) about the journey. He was the first person definitively documented to have sailed around the world alone.
questions.memory_hook: Slocum: solo around the world, 1895–1898. Three years, one man, one boat.

questions.question: What is the distance of a standard marathon in kilometers?
questions.explanation: A standard marathon is 42.195 kilometers (26.2 miles). The current world record is 2:00:35 set by Kelvin Kiptum of Kenya in 2023. Eliud Kipchoge broke the 2-hour barrier in an unofficial time trial in Vienna in 2019 with a time of 1:59:40.
questions.memory_hook: 42.195 km — the exact distance was set for Queen Alexandra in 1908.

questions.question: Muhammad Ali's refusal to be inducted into the US military in 1967 cost him three years of his prime fighting career. What were the consequences and ultimate outcome?
options: Ali was sentenced to five years in prison and served 18 months before being released; his boxing license was restored while he was still incarcerated, allowing him to train in prison

Ali was stripped of his heavyweight title and banned from boxing for three years; he was convicted of draft evasion (5 years prison), but the Supreme Court unanimously overturned his conviction in 1971
Ali was exiled from the US and fought exclusively in Europe and Africa for three years; he was pardoned by President Nixon in 1970 as part of a Vietnam War de-escalation strategy
Ali's draft refusal was actually legal — he had applied for conscientious objector status as a Muslim minister, and the ban on boxing was a commercial boycott by promoters, not a government action
questions.answer: Ali was stripped of his heavyweight title and banned from boxing for three years; he was convicted of draft evasion (5 years prison), but the Supreme Court unanimously overturned his conviction in 1971
questions.explanation: Ali stated: 'I ain't got no quarrel with them Viet Cong... No Viet Cong ever called me n***er.' He was immediately stripped of his boxing license in all 50 states, his passport was confiscated, and he was convicted of draft evasion and sentenced to 5 years. He appealed and stayed out of prison while the case wound through courts. In 1971, the Supreme Court unanimously reversed his conviction (8-0 with one abstention) on technical grounds related to the Army's mishandling of his conscientious objector application. He returned to boxing in 1970 and went on to regain the heavyweight title twice more.
questions.memory_hook: Ali said 'No Viet Cong ever called me n***er' and lost his title, passport, and boxing license for three years. The Supreme Court reversed his conviction unanimously in 1971 — the Army had mishandled his conscientious objector claim. He returned to regain the title twice more.

questions.question: Hip-hop is widely considered to have been born on August 11, 1973, at a party in the Bronx, New York. What happened that night, and why does it matter?
options: DJ Kool Herc invented the breakbeat by isolating the percussion section of a record and looping it using two turntables — creating the rhythmic foundation for all subsequent hip-hop music
Grandmaster Flash developed the 'scratching' technique for the first time at a Bronx block party, using vinyl records as musical instruments by manually moving them back and forth under the needle
Run-DMC performed their first show at a Bronx community center, combining hip-hop beats with rock guitar in a way that demonstrated rap music's commercial potential for mainstream audiences

Afrika Bambaataa founded the Universal Zulu Nation at a Bronx party, creating the organizational structure that would protect and promote hip-hop as a cultural movement rather than just a musical style
questions.answer: DJ Kool Herc invented the breakbeat by isolating the percussion section of a record and looping it using two turntables — creating the rhythmic foundation for all subsequent hip-hop music
questions.explanation: Herc (Clive Campbell), a Jamaican immigrant, threw a back-to-school party at 1520 Sedgwick Avenue in the South Bronx where he used two copies of the same record to extend the drum break ('Merry-Go-Round' technique). Dancers went wild during the break sections — those dancers became 'breakdancers' (b-boys). From this technical innovation came sampling, the DJ as performer, and the four elements of hip-hop culture: DJing, MCing, breakdancing, and graffiti. 1520 Sedgwick Avenue is now officially recognized as the birthplace of hip-hop by New York City.
questions.memory_hook: DJ Kool Herc invented the breakbeat on August 11, 1973 at a Bronx apartment party — two turntables, two copies of the same record, isolating the drum break and looping it. The dancers who went crazy during the break became 'b-boys.' 1520 Sedgwick Avenue is officially New York City's birthplace of hip-hop.

questions.question: Which band is known for 'Welcome to the Jungle' and 'November Rain'?
questions.explanation: Guns N' Roses released 'Welcome to the Jungle' and 'November Rain' from their landmark albums Appetite for Destruction (1987) and Use Your Illusion I (1991). Appetite for Destruction is the best-selling debut album in US history. The band's chaotic lineup has centered around guitarist Slash and vocalist Axl Rose.
questions.memory_hook: GN'R: jungle in '87, rain in '91. Slash's top hat and Axl's bandana.

questions.question: Billie Holiday's 1939 recording of 'Strange Fruit' — a graphic depiction of Southern lynching — is often cited as the first great protest song of the civil rights era. What made it so historically significant?
options: Strange Fruit was the first song to be broadcast on national radio with a political message — NBC initially refused to air it, and Holiday's fight to broadcast it became a landmark of First Amendment law

It confronted American audiences with the reality of racial terror in the most mainstream possible medium — popular music — at a time when lynching was still occurring and largely ignored by mainstream media and politics
Strange Fruit launched the commercial career of Mercury Records, the first record label founded by Black musicians, after major labels refused to record it due to its subject matter

Strange Fruit was secretly composed by the NAACP's legal team as part of a campaign to pass federal anti-lynching legislation, and Holiday was approached specifically because of her crossover appeal to white audiences
questions.answer: It confronted American audiences with the reality of racial terror in the most mainstream possible medium — popular music — at a time when lynching was still occurring and largely ignored by mainstream media and politics
questions.explanation: Abel Meeropol, a Jewish schoolteacher and communist activist, wrote the lyrics after seeing a photograph of a lynching. Holiday performed it at Café Society in New York, reportedly in absolute silence — no spotlight, no encore, no applause — the club darkened and a single spotlight on her face. Columbia Records refused to record it; she recorded it on Commodore Records. The Federal Bureau of Narcotics, headed by Harry Anslinger, targeted Holiday for years, possibly in part because of the song. Time magazine called it 'the song of the century' in 1999.
questions.memory_hook: Holiday performed Strange Fruit in total darkness — a single spotlight, absolute silence during the song, no encore. Columbia refused to record it. The song was later named 'song of the century' by Time. A federal drug agent reportedly targeted Holiday for years afterward, in part because of the song.

questions.question: Which event is known as the 'Shot Heard 'Round the World'?
options: The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand
The first battle of the American Revolution at Lexington
The dropping of the first atomic bomb
questions.answer: The first battle of the American Revolution at Lexington
questions.explanation: The phrase 'the shot heard 'round the world' comes from Ralph Waldo Emerson's 'Concord Hymn' (1837), referring to the first shots fired at the Battle of Lexington and Concord on April 19, 1775 — the opening engagement of the American Revolutionary War. The phrase has since been applied to other significant events.
questions.memory_hook: Lexington 1775: the shot that started a revolution that started a democracy.

questions.question: The Gobi Desert, one of the world's largest, spans which two countries?
questions.explanation: The Gobi Desert covers parts of northern and northwestern China and southern Mongolia, spanning about 1.3 million square kilometers. It is a cold desert — temperatures can drop to -40°C in winter. Unlike the Sahara, the Gobi has little sand; most of it is bare rock. It was a major part of the ancient Silk Road trade route.
questions.memory_hook: Gobi = China and Mongolia. Cold desert, not hot. Mostly rock, not sand. Ancient Silk Road ran through it. Dinosaur fossils were first discovered in the Gobi in the 1920s.

questions.question: Sound travels fastest through which medium?
questions.explanation: Sound travels through steel at about 5,120 meters per second, much faster than through water (about 1,480 m/s) or air (about 343 m/s). Sound cannot travel through a vacuum at all because it requires molecules to vibrate. Denser, more elastic materials transmit sound more efficiently — which is why you can hear a train coming by putting your ear to the rail.
questions.memory_hook: Sound travels fastest through solid steel. Steel > water > air > vacuum (no sound). Put your ear on a train rail and you hear it long before you'd hear it through air.

questions.question: The Yucatán Peninsula in southeastern Mexico contains thousands of cenotes — what are these natural features?
questions.explanation: Cenotes are natural sinkholes or caves filled with fresh water, formed when limestone bedrock collapses and exposes the groundwater below. The Yucatán has an extraordinary network of cenotes because its porous limestone platform has no surface rivers — all water flows underground. The ancient Maya considered cenotes sacred portals to the underworld (Xibalba) and used them for sacrificial offerings. A cenote impact site marks the Chicxulub crater — the asteroid that killed the dinosaurs 66 million years ago.
questions.memory_hook: Cenotes = freshwater sinkholes in Mexico's Yucatán. Limestone collapses → underground water exposed. No surface rivers — all water goes underground. Maya called them sacred portals. The Chicxulub impact crater that killed the dinosaurs is right there — cenotes ring its edge.

questions.question: Which pigment in plant leaves absorbs sunlight for photosynthesis and gives most plants their green color?
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll is the primary photosynthetic pigment in plants and algae. It absorbs red and blue light most efficiently, reflecting green light — which is why leaves appear green. Chlorophyll a is the main type; chlorophyll b is an accessory pigment. In autumn, chlorophyll breaks down as days shorten, revealing underlying yellow and orange carotenoid pigments (carotene, xanthophyll). Anthocyanins (red/purple) are produced fresh as protection against UV damage.
questions.memory_hook: Chlorophyll = green plant pigment that powers photosynthesis. Absorbs red + blue light, reflects green. Autumn leaves: chlorophyll breaks down, revealing yellow carotenes underneath. Red/purple leaves have anthocyanins for UV protection. All the color in leaves was always there — you just couldn't see it.
